title: Reasoning and Acting Combined
concept: Why interleaving reasoning thoughts with action tool calls outperforms planning only or acting only.

exercises.prompt: Quick start! An AI 'agent' is a program that can do what?

options.text: Only print text it was trained on, nothing else
Take steps and use tools to reach a goal
Store files on your computer's hard drive

exercises.explain: An AI agent doesn't just answer once. It works toward a goal by taking steps and using tools, like searching or calculating, along the way.

cards.title: Think, then do
cards.body: Imagine solving a puzzle. If you only plan without checking, you might miss a clue. If you only grab pieces without thinking, you flail. The best solvers do both, back and forth.

cards.title: What is ReAct?
cards.body: ReAct means 'Reasoning + Acting'. The AI alternates: it thinks a thought, then takes an action (like a search), sees the result, then thinks again. It loops until it has the answer.

cards.title: Like a detective
cards.body: A detective doesn't guess the whole case upfront. They form a hunch, check a clue, update the hunch, check another clue. Thinking and acting take turns until the mystery is solved.

cards.title: In action
cards.body: Question: 'Is it warmer today in Paris than yesterday?' Thought: I need today's temp. Action: check weather. Result: 18C. Thought: now I need yesterday's. Action: check history. Then it answers.

cards.title: Myth: plan it all first
cards.body: Some think a smart AI should plan every step before acting. But real tasks throw surprises. ReAct wins because it can adjust its plan the moment it sees a new result.

cards.title: Alone vs. combined
cards.body: Reasoning alone can invent facts. Acting alone has no strategy. ReAct blends them so each step is both smart and grounded in real results.

left.text: Thinks a lot, may guess facts it never checked

right.text: Thinks AND checks, correcting itself as it goes

exercises.prompt: Why does interleaving reasoning with actions beat doing just one?

options.text: Because it never needs any tools at all
Because thinking guides which action to take, and results correct the thinking
Because it makes the AI answer faster with fewer words
Because it removes the need to reach a goal

exercises.explain: The power comes from the loop: thoughts pick a smart next action, and the action's result feeds back to sharpen the next thought. Neither half works as well alone.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In ReAct, the AI decides its whole plan first and never changes it.
exercises.explain: False. ReAct's strength is that it updates its plan after each action's result, instead of locking in every step from the start.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word about the ReAct loop.
exercises.sentence: In ReAct, the AI takes turns between a reasoning thought and an action, using each result to guide the next thought.
exercises.explain: ReAct alternates a reasoning thought with an action, then uses the result to steer what it thinks next.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the AI-generated ReAct trace?

options.text: Thought: I need the capital of Japan. Action: search 'capital of Japan'. Result: Tokyo. Thought: I can answer now.
I already know everything, so I will not check anything and just talk forever.
Error 404: page could not be loaded, please try again later.

exercises.explain: The AI-generated trace shows the ReAct pattern: a thought, then an action, then a result, then a thought. The others aren't reasoning-plus-acting at all.

exercises.prompt: An agent must answer: 'Who directed the top movie at the box office last weekend?' Which approach fits ReAct best?

options.text: Guess a famous director from memory without checking anything
Think it needs the top movie, search for it, read the result, then think it needs the director and search again
Search 50 random things at once with no thinking between them
Write a full plan for every step and refuse to change it even if a search fails

exercises.explain: ReAct interleaves thinking and acting: first reason about what's missing, act to get it, then let that result shape the next thought. This beats pure guessing (no grounding) and pure random acting (no strategy). That back-and-forth loop is exactly why reasoning + acting outperforms either alone.
